chain_of_thought: Ghidra tools are unreachable, so I must reason from the symbolic stack only.
The first app-level native frame is Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum in libUbikeyJni.so, which sounds like an ASN.1 helper similar to OpenSSL’s internal Compute_ASN1_* routines.

The crash reason is just "Process Termination : Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum" with no explicit SIGSEGV/overflow; likely an internal bounds or consistency check abort inside ASN.1 parsing/serialization, not clearly memory-corruption at the crash site.

However, ASN.1 child-number computation routinely involves indexing into sequences/sets based on length/structure values parsed from attacker-controlled DER/ASN.1 blobs (here, presumably certificate data in CERT_Load / Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT).

The call-chain CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT → Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum is a classic parse pipeline for X.509 certs; in a real app this will be fed by untrusted network/server or token data, so inputs are plausibly attacker-controlled under realistic conditions.

Without decompilation I cannot see if Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum performs robust bounds checking or if the termination is due to a guard (e.g. explicit abort on malformed structure). But the fuzzer did cause a termination in this routine, which indicates at least an unhandled malformed ASN.1 state; many such bugs are memory-safety issues (OOB read/write, recursion overflows) but some are just "reject and abort".

The absence of a sanitizer/allocator frame suggests it is not clearly a hardened abort like stack protector; but the generic "Process Termination" wording could still be e.g. an assert or explicit exit().

Given only this stack, I cannot prove a specific unsafe operation (like memcpy) nor show that the bug goes beyond denial-of-service; but malformed ASN.1 child index computation driven by untrusted DER is a strong smell of a potential DoS at least (infinite loop, abort, integer error).

For classification, the question is whether to call this a genuine vulnerability. A crash in ASN.1 parsing of potentially attacker-controlled certs that leads to process termination is, at minimum, a DoS vulnerability if reachable in production; and the fuzz harness appears to call the real JNI entry Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetUser with fuzzer input instead of actual cert bytes.

Even without code, the realistic data-flow is: Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetUser (JNI) → CERT_Load (parse cert-like external data) → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT/TBS_CERT → Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum (where termination occurs); no intermediate validation layer is visible beyond those parsers.

Because I can’t see the implementation, I can’t assert memory corruption, so CWE-20/754-style malformed-input handling/DoS is a safer mapping than CWE-787. Severity is probably medium (DoS in authentication/user-lookup path), not high/critical RCE.

Confidence can’t be very high due to missing binary analysis, but the pattern (ASN.1 parsing crash on fuzzed input) is common and usually signals at least a robustness flaw reachable from untrusted inputs; I’ll rate it ~0.65 and call it vulnerable as a likely DoS / robustness bug, not proven memory corruption.
Limitations: no Ghidra/Jadx access, no Java call graph, no exact signal or instruction, so reasoning is mostly from symbol names and typical library semantics.
reasons: Crash occurs inside ASN.1/X.509 parsing helpers in libUbikeyJni.so when processing data passed via JNI, consistent with malformed-input driven failure.
Call chain CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT → Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum suggests attacker-controlled certificate/ASN.1 structures can trigger termination.
Lack of evidence for harness-only misuse or artificial NULLs implies a genuine robustness/DoS bug in ASN.1 child index computation, though memory corruption is unproven.

evidence.note: Top crashing frame in ASN.1 child-number computation, likely acting on parsed DER/X.509 structures.
Upstack frame indicating certificate loading/parsing pathway using ASN.1 helper routines.
JNI entry point invoked by fuzzer and app to perform user-related operations that involve certificate/ASN.1 processing.

recommendations: Disassemble and review Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, focusing on bounds checks for ASN.1 child indexing and length/offset calculations.
Fuzz CERT_Load and related ASN.1/X.509 parsers with structured ASN.1 corpora under ASan/UBSan to determine whether the crash is due to memory corruption or just an assert/abort.
Harden ASN.1 parsing: validate lengths and child counts before indexing, handle malformed structures gracefully by returning parse errors instead of terminating the process.
assumptions: Certificate/ASN.1 data processed by CERT_Load originates from untrusted or semi-untrusted sources in real app workflows.
The fuzzer is not injecting impossible JNI conditions (e.g., deliberate NULL env pointers) beyond supplying arbitrary input bytes to the normal jGetUser path.
Process termination arises from malformed ASN.1 data reaching Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum rather than external factors like timeouts or forced harness aborts.
limitations: Ghidra MCP backend is unreachable, so no direct decompilation of libUbikeyJni.so functions was possible.
No Java call graph or source was available to trace how inputs are constructed and passed into jGetUser.
Exact crash signal, instruction, and memory state at termination are unknown, so memory-corruption vs. logic-abort cannot be conclusively distinguished.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame before libc is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, which calls memcpy. Decompilation shows __size is derived from ASN.1 length fields in param_1 (DER data). For long-form length, it parses length bytes into uVar7, checks if (int)uVar7 < 0 then bails, otherwise sets __size to (int)uVar7. For short form, __size = (size_t)bVar2 (second byte). Then allocates malloc(__size) and memcpy(__dest, param_1 + iVar10, __size). No check that __size fits within the actual buffer behind param_1; param_1 is treated as a flat pointer with no bounds. Under fuzzing, param_1 is likely just a buffer of size iVar2 bytes received from Java. If __size exceeds iVar2 - iVar10, memcpy will read out of bounds, causing crash. Upstream, Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetUser obtains length iVar2 from JNI env function (GetArrayLength-style) and __src pointer from another JNI function (GetByteArrayElements/critical), then allocates a heap buffer of exactly that size, copies iVar2 bytes into it, and wraps it into a BINSTR. CERT_Load then passes that BINSTR (probably struct with pointer+len) into Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, but Decode_DER_ASN1_ST receives only the raw pointer (BINSTR data) and not its length. There is no guarding of Decode_DER_ASN1_ST by the BINSTR length; CERT_Load simply calls Decode_DER_ASN1_ST(*param_2) where *param_2 is some pointer (likely start of DER). Hence an attacker controlling the input byte[] to jGetUser can craft ASN.1 that advertises a huge length causing OOB read in memcpy. This is classical unauthenticated ASN.1 length parsing bug.

Crash is in __memcpy according to Process Termination. Data-flow from JNI shows attacker-controlled Java byte array (user or remote) maps to __src/__size. The memcpy in JNI (memcpy(__s,__src,__size)) is safe because __size equals iVar2, the true length, and __s was allocated with malloc(__size). Crash more likely in memcpy inside Decode_DER_ASN1_ST where __size is arbitrary ASN.1 length. There is no high-level Java validation available (empty Java callgraph) but JNI code takes arbitrary blob and feeds to CERT_Load directly. Fuzz harness fuzzes fuzz_one_input which presumably calls this JNI, so harness is simulating realistic attacker control. This makes the issue a genuine, reachable memory corruption/out-of-bounds read.

Type of vulnerability: Out-of-bounds read in ASN.1 decoder due to trusting length fields without underlying buffer length. This can cause crashes and potentially infoleak or even heap corruption if later code writes based on those sizes. Here it only reads into malloc(__size) then uses that buffer, so direct overflow is on source side, but memcpy with oversized length can still run into unmapped memory or cross into other heap objects; that is still memory safety. Likely at least DoS, potentially more if combined with other operations on malformed ASN.1. Severity: high due to memory corruption from attacker-controlled certificate/DER data in crypto-like code path.

JNI entrypoint and native library are application code (libUbikeyJni.so), not harness-only. Nothing suggests this code is debug-only or unreachable. The lack of boundary checks in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, combined with clear attacker-controlled taint from Java/com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetUser, is strong evidence of a true vulnerability rather than harness artifact.
reasons: Decode_DER_ASN1_ST computes memcpy length directly from ASN.1 length bytes without any check against actual buffer size
JNI method Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetUser accepts arbitrary Java byte array, copies it, and passes it to CERT_Load/Decode_DER_ASN1_ST without further validation
Crash occurs in __memcpy consistent with out-of-bounds read on attacker-controlled DER/ASN.1 data
Code resides in app library libUbikeyJni.so and is reachable from a straightforward JNI path, not a fuzz-only stub

evidence.note: __size is derived from ASN.1 length bytes in param_1; no check that __size fits within the actual DER buffer
Length computation only checks sign of uVar7, not whether this many bytes are available in input buffer
JNI copies attacker-controlled Java byte[] into heap buffer and passes as-is into certificate loader without enforcing any constraints on internal ASN.1 lengths
CERT_Load passes only pointer *param_2 (likely BINSTR data) to Decode_DER_ASN1_ST without passing its length; decoder cannot enforce bounds.

recommendations: In Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, pass and enforce the total buffer length; before memcpy, ensure __size <= available_bytes (buffer_len - offset) to avoid OOB read
Harden ASN.1 parsing by using a well-tested library or central length-checking helpers instead of manual arithmetic on DER length bytes
Add defensive validation in JNI jGetUser (e.g., maximum input length, certificate size caps) and comprehensive tests/fuzzing around CERT_Load/Decode_DER_ASN1_ST
assumptions: param_1 to Decode_DER_ASN1_ST ultimately points to the user-controlled DER buffer originating from Java byte[]
*param_2 in CERT_Load is (or contains) a pointer to that DER buffer without implicit larger backing store than the Java array
The reported crash in __memcpy corresponds to the memcpy inside Decode_DER_ASN1_ST rather than the earlier safe memcpy in JNI
limitations: Java-side call graph to jGetUser not available, so exact origin of its byte[] input is inferred
Exact BINSTR structure definition is not decompiled here; interpretation of *param_2 as pointer to DER data is based on typical patterns and naming
Fuzz harness details are not shown, so precise triggering input for this crash is unknown, though path is realistic

chain_of_thought: Identify first app-level native frame: top frame is __memcpy (allocator/libc); next is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST in libUbikeyJni.so, which we decompiled. Crash reason is Process Termination: __memcpy, implying memcpy hit invalid src/dst or size.

In Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, memcpy(__dest,param_1 + iVar10,__size) copies from param_1 (ASN.1 DER buffer) into a freshly malloc’d buffer of size __size. __dest is guaranteed to be malloc(__size), so dst is safe as long as malloc succeeds (checked). Potential issue is that __size is derived from DER length fields without any bound check vs actual buffer length, and memcpy src pointer param_1 + iVar10 can go out of bounds if __size exceeds remaining input size. However, we must see how param_1 is provided.

Decode_DER_ASN1_ST is called from CERT_Load: lVar3 = Decode_DER_ASN1_ST(*param_2). *param_2 is passed from caller as an 8-byte value that is treated as pointer to ASN.1 DER bytes. No length is provided to Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, so it assumes the buffer is large enough as implied by encoded length. No internal bounds checking against an explicit size exists. Thus Decode_DER_ASN1_ST is an inherently unsafe decoder; if the pointer or backing buffer is attacker-controlled and may be short, this is a memory corruption bug (out-of-bounds read in memcpy).

Need to see how CERT_Load is called from JNI: in Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetUser, function gets a JNIEnv* (param_1), jclass/jobject (param_2), and some jbyteArray/jstring handle param_3. It calls JNIEnv function at offset 0x558 to get length: iVar2 = (*env->GetArrayLength)(env,param_3). __size = (size_t)iVar2. Then __src = (*env->GetByteArrayElements or Get...)(env,param_3,0). Then __s = malloc(__size); memset(__s,0,__size); memcpy(__s,__src,__size); releases original elements via (*env->ReleaseByteArrayElements)(...,2). So __s is an exact in-memory copy of the Java byte array, size equal to array length. BINSTR_Create and BINSTR_SetData(__s,iVar2,&local_40) wrap that into BINSTR; CERT_Load(&local_48,&local_40) then uses that BINSTR as param_2 (pointer to data).

We did not see BINSTR_* implementations, but CERT_Load prototype takes param_2 as undefined8* and dereferences *param_2 directly as pointer passed into Decode_DER_ASN1_ST. Given how BINSTR_SetData is likely to work, it almost certainly stores a pointer and length, but CERT_Load ignores length and only uses the pointer. Within Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, the ASN.1 DER decoder uses internal length fields to decide malloc sizes and memcpy size. There is no guarantee that those fields match the Java array length; a malicious caller from Java could supply arbitrary bytes with a bogus length field making __size huge or negative (interpreted int), triggering out-of-bounds read in memcpy, possibly segfaulting or reading past buffer into adjacent heap memory.

Because this is called from JNI jGetUser which is exported and used by the real app, and it takes a Java byte array derived from untrusted sources (e.g. server or NFC token) with no validation except ASN.1 decoding, the crash under fuzzing is highly indicative of a real memory safety bug: out-of-bounds read due to trusting internal ASN.1 length fields without length parameter. memcpy using arbitrary __size from attacker-controlled DER and src pointer to fixed buffer is a classic CWE-125/787. Even though only read occurs (dst is newly malloced, src is in-bounds but possibly too small), memcpy with oversized length reads memory beyond array, causing access violation or info leak if not crashing. In native code on Android, such decoding often happens on arbitrary user certificates or tokens. Fuzzer causing __memcpy crash matches this.

There is no indication that harness passes impossible values; JNI code uses official JNIEnv methods and copies the array; fuzz harness just feeds param_3 contents and length like real Java would. So this is not a harness artefact but a true bug in ASN.1 decoder lacking bounds checking against actual input size.
Therefore classify as vulnerable, high severity memory safety (out-of-bounds read, potential DoS and info leak; depending on allocator, could also lead to heap issues though here only read).
reasons: Decode_DER_ASN1_ST uses memcpy with length (__size) derived from ASN.1 length fields without any bounds check against the actual buffer size
CERT_Load and JNI wrapper Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetUser pass only a pointer to user-controlled DER buffer; no length is supplied or checked by the decoder
Crash occurs in __memcpy consistent with out-of-bounds read when attacker supplies DER with bogus length fields that exceed the Java array length

evidence.note: __size is computed from encoded length fields; no check against actual buffer length for param_1, risking out-of-bounds read.
Passes only a pointer *param_2 to DER data into decoder; no length argument, so decoder cannot enforce bounds.
JNI bridge takes Java byte array of attacker-controlled length, copies it, and passes through without any validation other than later failing in ASN.1 decode.

recommendations: In Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, pass and enforce an explicit buffer length parameter; verify that computed __size and iVar10 do not exceed remaining input size before memcpy
Add robust ASN.1 parsing with bounds checks (or use a vetted library) instead of trusting length fields directly
At the JNI layer, validate that the DER structure length fields are sane relative to the Java byte array length and reject malformed inputs before decoding
assumptions: BINSTR_SetData stores both pointer and length but CERT_Load/Decode_DER_ASN1_ST ignore length and only use the pointer
Java-composed byte array for jGetUser can be influenced by untrusted sources in typical app use
limitations: Implementations of BINSTR_Create/BINSTR_SetData were not inspected, so exact representation of param_2 in CERT_Load is inferred
Scope of exploitation (pure OOB read vs further heap issues) depends on allocator and platform behaviour not visible from decompilation
